q: According to Newton's second law, an object that is accelerating must have a net force acting on it.
In uniform circular motion, the speed is variable.
The centripetal acceleration is directed away from the centre of the circle at any moment.
In the absence of a centripetal force, an object in circular motion would move along a straight line.
The magnitude of the centripetal force can be written as $F_c = mr\omega^2$.
The string pulls inward on the ball to provide the required centripetal force.
The gravitational force of the earth on the moon is an example of a centripetal force.
The electric force acting on an electron orbiting the nucleus is directed away from the nucleus.
A tension force behaves as a centripetal force when a body attached to a string is whirled.
An attractive force exists between the sun and the earth in a direction parallel to the path of the earth.
The Coulomb attractive force between the nucleus and the electron acts as the required centrifugal force.
When a car turns in a curved path, the frictional force acts normally to the direction of motion of the car.
When a car moves on an inclined circular path, the vertical component of the normal force provides the required centripetal force.
The upward thrust on an aeroplane body acts normally to the aeroplane body.
On an unbanked road, the centripetal force is supplied by the normal force.
If the frictional force is not sufficient under wet conditions, the car will skid out of the circular path.
Banking of roads is raising the inner edge of the curved road higher than the outer edge.
The angle that the inclined track makes with the horizontal is called the angle of friction.
The normal force $F_N$ exerted by a banked road is perpendicular to the road.
For every banked curve, there is a limiting speed at which the entire centripetal force is supplied by the horizontal component of the normal force.
When a car takes a turn at the limiting speed on a banked curve, the frictional force between the tires and the road is not required.
The equation for the banking angle without friction is $\tan \theta = \frac{v^2}{rg}$.
Since there is no vertical motion for a car on a banked road, the net force in the y direction is zero.
Centrifugal force acts towards the centre of the circular path.
Except centripetal force, there is another real force acting on a whirled ball along the string.
The outward force felt by a person in a car moving around a curve is caused by the effect of inertia.
Centrifugal force is a real force.
The centrifugal force is equal and opposite to the centripetal force.
In a laboratory centrifuge, the radial acceleration causes less dense particles to settle to the bottom of the tube.
The sedimentation principle states that low density substances rise to the top in a centrifuge.
